title: Earnings for those with tertiary are higher and grow faster
subtitle: Wage profiles by worker experience
source: World Bank staff calculations, based on data of GLD (Global Labor Database Repository), World Bank. Wages in PPP adjusted US dollars.
visdescription: Line charts showing wage profiles for workers with differing levels of education for India and Brazil.

scenes.text: To see how learning (as measured through on-the-job productivity) evolves over a career, let us take a look at workers in [emphasis: India]. For workers at the beginning of their career, those who did not complete primary education earned around $1.70 per hour in 2019. These wages are higher for those with more experience, roughly doubling to $3.27 per hour by the end of the career.
In comparison, workers at the start of their careers with a tertiary degree are already earning around 75% more than their counterparts who did not complete primary education.

Over time, this wage gap continues to grow. By the end of their careers, workers with a tertiary degree have more than triple the wages as workers at the beginning, and earn roughly three times more than those who did not complete primary education.

[emphasis: Brazil ]shows a similar, but even more pronounced, divergence. Entry hourly wages for those who did not complete primary education are $2.43, while those with a tertiary degree start at $6.03. After a 35-year career, tertiary graduate workers earn about $24.32 per hour, which is around six times the $4 earned by those who did not complete primary education.
